EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed north of Kearney and moved east through Grand Island.  Strong winds, over 75 mph, caused crop and tree damage in the area. The fire was limited to only the attic and was quickly extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in central Nebraska and moved south.  These storms dropped hail up to the size of tennis balls and produced winds up to 80 mph.  Damage to crops and property extended from near Ord to near Doniphan.  Brief weak tornadoes were reported near Rockville and near Doniphan.  A stronger tornado set down near Cario and moved southeast.  A farmhouse was severely damaged.  The owner of the home escaped injury by taking shelter in the basement.  Other storms developed in south-central Nebraska near Arapahoe.  These storms also produced large hail and strong winds.  Several reports of funnel were received but none reached the ground.

It was estimated that there was 1.5 million dollars damage to the corn crop and 1.5 million dollars damage to the soybean crop. Other significant crop damage occurred in the Coleridge area and to the north of Randolph.

Winds gusting to 45 mph combined with 1 to 3 inches of snow and existing loose snow cover to produce blizzard conditions with frequent zero visibilities. Travel and commerce were temporarily brought to a halt, with the zero visibility and roads closed by drifting.

A slow moving area of snow totaled 3 to 6 inches of snow over a small part of South Central Nebraska.  Nuckolls and Thayer counties received the most snow, generally in excess of 6 inches.  Hebron measured 9 inches of fresh snow.  A few schools closed early because of snow and several minor accidents occurred on the slick roads.

The first significant snowfall in several weeks occurred the night of February 3 in South Central Nebraska.  By the morning of the 4th, as much as 10 inches of new snow had fallen in Greeley.  North Loup reported 7 inches of snow. An weak upper level low pressure system teamed with some low level moisture and blanketed much of South Central Nebraska with 2 to 5 inches of snow.  The town of Oxford recorded 5.3 inches of new snow from the storm.  Numerous minor accidents were reported with the ice and snow covered roads.

Severe thunderstorms developed in central Nebraska and moved south.  These storms dropped hail up to the size of tennis balls and produced winds up to 80 mph.  Damage to crops and property extended from near Ord to near Doniphan.  Brief weak tornadoes were reported near Rockville and near Doniphan.  A stronger tornado set down near Cario and moved southeast.  A farmhouse was severely damaged.  The owner of the home escaped injury by taking shelter in the basement.  Other storms developed in south-central Nebraska near Arapahoe.  These storms also produced large hail and strong winds.  Several reports of funnel were received but none reached the ground.

Snow showers did occur with the wind, but the low visibility was mainly caused by blowing snow.

A large area of western Kimball County received in excess of 5 inches of rain in less than 5 hours, with the remainder of the county and western Cheyenne County averaging more than 2 inches.  The maximum rainfall reported was 7. 5 inches in the southwest corner of the county.  Cropland flooding was widespread throughout both counties, and Lodgepole Creek spilled out of its banks from Bushnell in western Kimball County to Lodgepole in extreme eastern Cheyenne County, affecting several communities.  Two bridges were washed out along Lodgepole Creek--one on a county road north of Dix in eastern Kimball County, and the second on Highway 30 outside of Potter in western Cheyenne County.  While flood waters were reported in Bushnell and Lodgepole, the most severe occurred in Potter, where 4 feet of water inundated numerous homes and businesses, washing away a dozen large propane tanks and washing out 30 feet of Union Pacific Railroad track serving as a dike on Lodgepole Creek.  Numerous county roads in both counties were damaged or washed out, and more than 80 miles of farmland along Lodgepole Creek and Sand Draw were  covered with several feet of flood water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping Pong ball sized hail fell in southwest Kimball County.

An weak upper level low pressure system teamed with some low level moisture and blanketed much of South Central Nebraska with 2 to 5 inches of snow.  The town of Oxford recorded 5.3 inches of new snow from the storm.  Numerous minor accidents were reported with the ice and snow covered roads.

Mild temperatures melted snowcover in a short time period allowing for a sudden runoff into streams and rivers which were still intact with thicker than normal ice.  The runoff into the streams and rivers was sufficient to break up the ice.  Ice jams formed at many locations causing flooding.  Serious flooding occurred on the lower Loup and lower Platte rivers.  The strongest and most urban flooding occurred in the stretch of the Platte river along Saunders and Sarpy counties between the mouth of the Elkhorn river to the State Highway 6 bridge near Linoma Beach/Beacon View.  This was the result of a 2.5 mile long ice jam along the Platte river.  Thomas Lakes was severely impacted with ice jam flooding.  Beacon View homes were also flooded.  Several structures at Camp Ashland were also flooded forcing the evacuation of 150 people.  Some three thousand acres of farmlands in western Sarpy county were flooded as the local levee was overtopped upstream of Beacon View.  Likewise, flooding entered the flood plain on the Saunders county side of the Platte.  Two to three days of dynamiting of the Platte river ice jam near Beacon View beginning on the 20th did much to bust up the strongest ice jam that acted as the main blockage in the area.  The Ponca creek in Knox county flooded considerable lowlands.  Many county roads and State Highway 12 near Verdel flooded.  Bazile creek also flooded lowlands and State Highway 12 for a brief time period.  The Bazile and Ponca creeks also ran out of their banks on the east and west sides of Niobrara.   The Verdigre creek near Verdigre ran out if its banks due to ice jamming.  This caused the evacuation of three homes in Verdigre.Other lowland flooding occurred along the Logan creek near Uehling, the Maple creek near Nickerson, and the Loup river north of Columbus, and the Elkhorn river near West Point.

The first significant snowfall in several weeks occurred the night of February 3 in South Central Nebraska.  By the morning of the 4th, as much as 10 inches of new snow had fallen in Greeley.  North Loup reported 7 inches of snow. Amounts throughout South Central Nebraska generally ranged from 1 to 4 inches, but new snow totals of 5 to 8 were common north and west of the Grand Island and Kearney areas.  A rash of accidents was reported in the area on the snow and ice covered roads the morning of the 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved south from Canada through the central plains as low pressure moved east along the U.S./Canadian border into southeast Canada.  Strong winds gusted over 60 mph.  This produced considerable blowing and drifting snow with visibilities reduced to less than 1/4 mile in open areas.  Visibilities were not as low in sheltered areas, although there was still extensive blowing and drifting.  Snow showers did occur with the wind, but the low visibility was mainly caused by blowing snow.
Strong northwest winds combined with cold temperatures to generate wind chills of 40 to 50 below zero.
Whiteout conditions created by blowing snow caused a two-vehicle collision 15 miles south of Chadron on Nebraska Highway 385.  A 13-year-old female was hospitalized as a result.

Mild temperatures melted snowcover in a short time period allowing for a sudden runoff into streams and rivers which were still intact with thicker than normal ice.  The runoff into the streams and rivers was sufficient to break up the ice.  Ice jams formed at many locations causing flooding.  Serious flooding occurred on the lower Loup and lower Platte rivers.  The strongest and most urban flooding occurred in the stretch of the Platte river along Saunders and Sarpy counties between the mouth of the Elkhorn river to the State Highway 6 bridge near Linoma Beach/Beacon View.  This was the result of a 2.5 mile long ice jam along the Platte river.  Thomas Lakes was severely impacted with ice jam flooding.  Beacon View homes were also flooded.  Several structures at Camp Ashland were also flooded forcing the evacuation of 150 people.  Some three thousand acres of farmlands in western Sarpy county were flooded as the local levee was overtopped upstream of Beacon View.  Likewise, flooding entered the flood plain on the Saunders county side of the Platte.  Two to three days of dynamiting of the Platte river ice jam near Beacon View beginning on the 20th did much to bust up the strongest ice jam that acted as the main blockage in the area.  The Ponca creek in Knox county flooded considerable lowlands.  Many county roads and State Highway 12 near Verdel flooded.  Bazile creek also flooded lowlands and State Highway 12 for a brief time period.  The Bazile and Ponca creeks also ran out of their banks on the east and west sides of Niobrara.   The Verdigre creek near Verdigre ran out if its banks due to ice jamming.  This caused the evacuation of three homes in Verdigre.Other lowland flooding occurred along the Logan creek near Uehling, the Maple creek near Nickerson, and the Loup river north of Columbus, and the Elkhorn river near West Point.

Winds gusting to 45 mph combined with 1 to 3 inches of snow and existing loose snow cover to produce blizzard conditions with frequent zero visibilities. Travel and commerce were temporarily brought to a halt, with the zero visibility and roads closed by drifting.

Strong winds gusting as high as 50 mph combined with loose snow cover and 1 to 3 inches of new snow to produce prolonged blizzard conditions. Transportation, schools, and commerce were halted as many roads were blocked by drifting and zero visibility made travel otherwise impossible. The greatest damage was suspected to be long term economic loss from the loss of transportation, commerce, and farm services; and the amount of this damage could not be accurately estimated.

A record breaking late season cold snap generated the latest spring freeze on record over portions of eastern Nebraska as temperatures dipped into the 26-32F degree range.  Some minor crop and fruit tree damage resulted from the cold temperatures.  This was especially true in low lying areas since the cold air tended to drain into those areas.  Damage was generally minor as many of the plants had just begun to emerge and were not particularly succeptible to the cold.

Strong winds combined with snow amounts of generally 8 to 12 inches to create widespread blizzard conditions in the Nebraska panhandle.  Closed and impassable roads became the norm from the evening of April 4 through April 6 due to blowing and drifting snow.  Many automobile accidents occurred as a result of the treacherous conditions, and many power lines were downed due to the weight of snow.  Many livestock were killed by the snow and accompanying cold temperatures because the storm occurred in the calving season.  The highest snow amount was 12 inches in the Wildcat Hills region of Banner County, and the strongest wind recorded was 68 MPH at Sidney at 900 PM April 5.

Heavy snow fell over central and southern portions of the Panhandle.  Accumulations of 7 to 9 inches were widespread.  A localized band of heavier snow remained nearly stationary across Banner County and western Morrill County which resulted in  accumulations of 10 inches at Harrisburg and Bayard.

A record breaking late season cold snap generated the latest spring freeze on record over portions of eastern Nebraska as temperatures dipped into the 26-32F degree range.  Some minor crop and fruit tree damage resulted from the cold temperatures.  This was especially true in low lying areas since the cold air tended to drain into those areas.  Damage was generally minor as many of the plants had just begun to emerge and were not particularly succeptible to the cold.

Surface low pressure developed and strengthened over northern Texas then moved northeast through Missouri and into Illinois.  This combined with a strong upper level low pressure trough which moved out of the Rockies and into the plains generated widespread snow across the region.  Snow fell particularly heavy from mid morning through mid afternoon April 11th over southern Boone...northern Platte... southeast Madison...Stanton...northern Colfax...Cuming...southern Thurston and northern Burt counties.  Snow then decreased during the late afternoon hours, then increased by early evening and continued through much of the night as the main upper level system moved northeast from western Kansas through Nebraska.  By morning April 12th, most of the area had experienced 5 to 9 inches of snow from the system.

No injuries were reported.

Thunderstorms along Interstate 80 produced a variety of weather across south-central Nebraska.  Winds from 60 to 75 mph along with hail up to an inch and a half in diameter causes considerable crop and property damage.  Several funnel clouds were spotted in Dawson County.  A well developed wall cloud and a few funnels occurred in Merrick County.

45-55 mph winds created blizzard conditions across northcentral Nebraska.  Blowing and drifting snow caused icy roads and visibilities near zero. A storm-related fatality occurred when an 18 year-old male was struck and killed by a passing vehicle.

A strong low pressure system moved northeast from the central plains through northern Minnesota to eastern Canada.  The tight pressure gradient between the low and a cold Canadian high pressure system which moved south into the plains behind the low generated very strong winds.  Winds gusted over 60 mph over the area.  Some trees were blown down and scattered power outages occurred.

Mild temperatures melted snowcover in a short time period allowing for a sudden runoff into streams and rivers which were still intact with thicker than normal ice.  The runoff into the streams and rivers was sufficient to break up the ice.  Ice jams formed at many locations causing flooding.  Serious flooding occurred on the lower Loup and lower Platte rivers.  The strongest and most urban flooding occurred in the stretch of the Platte river along Saunders and Sarpy counties between the mouth of the Elkhorn river to the State Highway 6 bridge near Linoma Beach/Beacon View.  This was the result of a 2.5 mile long ice jam along the Platte river.  Thomas Lakes was severely impacted with ice jam flooding.  Beacon View homes were also flooded.  Several structures at Camp Ashland were also flooded forcing the evacuation of 150 people.  Some three thousand acres of farmlands in western Sarpy county were flooded as the local levee was overtopped upstream of Beacon View.  Likewise, flooding entered the flood plain on the Saunders county side of the Platte.  Two to three days of dynamiting of the Platte river ice jam near Beacon View beginning on the 20th did much to bust up the strongest ice jam that acted as the main blockage in the area.  The Ponca creek in Knox county flooded considerable lowlands.  Many county roads and State Highway 12 near Verdel flooded.  Bazile creek also flooded lowlands and State Highway 12 for a brief time period.  The Bazile and Ponca creeks also ran out of their banks on the east and west sides of Niobrara.   The Verdigre creek near Verdigre ran out if its banks due to ice jamming.  This caused the evacuation of three homes in Verdigre.Other lowland flooding occurred along the Logan creek near Uehling, the Maple creek near Nickerson, and the Loup river north of Columbus, and the Elkhorn river near West Point.

Low pressure moved northeast from Oklahoma through Missouri and into Illinois.  This combined with a strong upper level disturbance which moved out of the southwestern U.S. into the central plains to generate four to seven inches of snow.

The first significant snowfall in several weeks occurred the night of February 3 in South Central Nebraska.  By the morning of the 4th, as much as 10 inches of new snow had fallen in Greeley.  North Loup reported 7 inches of snow. Amounts throughout South Central Nebraska generally ranged from 1 to 4 inches, but new snow totals of 5 to 8 were common north and west of the Grand Island and Kearney areas.  A rash of accidents was reported in the area on the snow and ice covered roads the morning of the 4th.

An overnight snow dumped a narrow band of heavy snow along and just south of the Platte River valley in South Central Nebraska.  Snow totals of 6 to 9 inches were common from Elwood and Holdrege to Polk.  Aurora and Atlanta reported 9 inches of snow from the storm.  Several schools were closed or started late the morning of February 24.Lighter snow amounts, from 1 to 4 inches, were recorded along the periphery of the main snow band.

This caused the evacuation of three homes in Verdigre.Other lowland flooding occurred along the Logan creek near Uehling, the Maple creek near Nickerson, and the Loup river north of Columbus, and the Elkhorn river near West Point.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gustnado 1SW of Bushnell I-80 interchange

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mild temperatures melted snowcover in a short time period allowing for a sudden runoff into streams and rivers which were still intact with thicker than normal ice.  The runoff into the streams and rivers was sufficient to break up the ice.  Ice jams formed at many locations causing flooding.  Serious flooding occurred on the lower Loup and lower Platte rivers.  The strongest and most urban flooding occurred in the stretch of the Platte river along Saunders and Sarpy counties between the mouth of the Elkhorn river to the State Highway 6 bridge near Linoma Beach/Beacon View.  This was the result of a 2.5 mile long ice jam along the Platte river.  Thomas Lakes was severely impacted with ice jam flooding.  Beacon View homes were also flooded.  Several structures at Camp Ashland were also flooded forcing the evacuation of 150 people.  Some three thousand acres of farmlands in western Sarpy county were flooded as the local levee was overtopped upstream of Beacon View.  Likewise, flooding entered the flood plain on the Saunders county side of the Platte.  Two to three days of dynamiting of the Platte river ice jam near Beacon View beginning on the 20th did much to bust up the strongest ice jam that acted as the main blockage in the area.  The Ponca creek in Knox county flooded considerable lowlands.  Many county roads and State Highway 12 near Verdel flooded.  Bazile creek also flooded lowlands and State Highway 12 for a brief time period.  The Bazile and Ponca creeks also ran out of their banks on the east and west sides of Niobrara.   The Verdigre creek near Verdigre ran out if its banks due to ice jamming.  This caused the evacuation of three homes in Verdigre.Other lowland flooding occurred along the Logan creek near Uehling, the Maple creek near Nickerson, and the Loup river north of Columbus, and the Elkhorn river near West Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure developed and strengthened over northern Texas then moved northeast through Missouri and into Illinois.  This combined with a strong upper level low pressure trough which moved out of the Rockies and into the plains generated widespread snow across the region.  As warm, moist air was forced to overrun cold arctic air over the area, snow developed during the late evening of April 10th with 4-6 inches of accumulation by 6am CST on April 11th.  Snow was light for much of the morning and early afternoon.  Heavy snow then increased again during the late afternoon and continued through much of the night as the main upper level system moved northeast from western Kansas through Nebraska.  By morning April 12th, most of the area had experienced between 8 and 12 inches of snow from the system.

A strong arctic cold front moved south from Canada through the central plains as low pressure moved east along the U.S./Canadian border into southeast Canada.  Strong winds gusted over 60 mph.  This produced considerable blowing and drifting snow with visibilities reduced to less than 1/4 mile in open areas.  Visibilities were not as low in sheltered areas, although there was still extensive blowing and drifting.  Snow showers did occur with the wind, but the low visibility was mainly caused by blowing snow.

Several days of temperatures above freezing caused snow to melt and ice to break up on Ponca Creek and Keya Paha River in northcentral Nebraska.  The extra runoff from snowmelt combined with ice jams along the waterways caused flooding from Bristow east to the Boyd County line.  In Keya Paha County flooding occurred near the community of Burton.  Numerous homes and businesses incurred damage.  County roads and bridges also sustained damage with Keya Paha County reporting $286,701 damage to its roads and bridges.  Also, considerable soil erosion occurred to agricultural land.

A strong low pressure system moved northeast from the central plains through northern Minnesota to eastern Canada.  The tight pressure gradient between the low and a cold Canadian high pressure system which moved south into the plains behind the low generated very strong winds.  Winds gusted to 60 mph over the area.  Some trees were blown down and scattered power outages occurred.

A record breaking late season cold snap generated the latest spring freeze on record over portions of eastern Nebraska as temperatures dipped into the 26-32F degree range.  Some minor crop and fruit tree damage resulted from the cold temperatures.  This was especially true in low lying areas since the cold air tended to drain into those areas.  Damage was generally minor as many of the plants had just begun to emerge and were not particularly succeptible to the cold.

An weak upper level low pressure system teamed with some low level moisture and blanketed much of South Central Nebraska with 2 to 5 inches of snow.  The town of Oxford recorded 5.3 inches of new snow from the storm.  Numerous minor accidents were reported with the ice and snow covered roads.

An overnight snow dumped a narrow band of heavy snow along and just south of the Platte River valley in South Central Nebraska.  Snow totals of 6 to 9 inches were common from Elwood and Holdrege to Polk.  Aurora and Atlanta reported 9 inches of snow from the storm.  Several schools were closed or started late the morning of February 24.Lighter snow amounts, from 1 to 4 inches, were recorded along the periphery of the main snow band.

The first significant snowfall in several weeks occurred the night of February 3 in South Central Nebraska.  By the morning of the 4th, as much as 10 inches of new snow had fallen in Greeley.  North Loup reported 7 inches of snow. Amounts throughout South Central Nebraska generally ranged from 1 to 4 inches, but new snow totals of 5 to 8 were common north and west of the Grand Island and Kearney areas.  A rash of accidents was reported in the area on the snow and ice covered roads the morning of the 4th.

A disturbance in the upper atmosphere moved quickly across a part of South Central Nebraska producing a brief period of freezing rain and depositing a layer of ice on local roads.   Near Aurora, a two car accident occurred when the driver of an eastbound car lost control, crossed the median and hit a west bound vehicle head on.  Near that accident, another driver lost control of his vehicle, slid into the median and into a crossover road.  No serious injuries were reported in either case.

Schools were closed Thursday and Friday. Track meets were postponed to a later date. Several vehicle accidents were reported but no injuries were involved.
Six branches 3 inches in diameter downed.

A large area of western Kimball County received in excess of 5 inches of rain in less than 5 hours, with the remainder of the county and western Cheyenne County averaging more than 2 inches.  The maximum rainfall reported was 7. 5 inches in the southwest corner of the county.  Cropland flooding was widespread throughout both counties, and Lodgepole Creek spilled out of its banks from Bushnell in western Kimball County to Lodgepole in extreme eastern Cheyenne County, affecting several communities.  Two bridges were washed out along Lodgepole Creek--one on a county road north of Dix in eastern Kimball County, and the second on Highway 30 outside of Potter in western Cheyenne County.  While flood waters were reported in Bushnell and Lodgepole, the most severe occurred in Potter, where 4 feet of water inundated numerous homes and businesses, washing away a dozen large propane tanks and washing out 30 feet of Union Pacific Railroad track serving as a dike on Lodgepole Creek.  Numerous county roads in both counties were damaged or washed out, and more than 80 miles of farmland along Lodgepole Creek and Sand Draw were  covered with several feet of flood water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding occurred along Lodgepole Creek and Sand Draw from heavy rains upstream in Kimball County.  A 7 foot wall of water washed out a 30 foot section of  a bridge on U.S. Highway 30 at Potter at 430 am MST.
Brief touchdown occurred 3SSE of I-80 mile post 18 in open country.

Widespread flash flooding then flooding with crop, road and bridge damage.  All county roads were closed from 1900 MST 6/2/97 until 0500 MST 6/3/97.  Minor to heavy silt damage was recorded on 40% of county roads.

Strong winds combined with snow amounts of generally 8 to 12 inches to create widespread blizzard conditions in the Nebraska panhandle.  Closed and impassable roads became the norm from the evening of April 4 through April 6 due to blowing and drifting snow.  Many automobile accidents occurred as a result of the treacherous conditions, and many power lines were downed due to the weight of snow.  Many livestock were killed by the snow and accompanying cold temperatures because the storm occurred in the calving season.  The highest snow amount was 12 inches in the Wildcat Hills region of Banner County, and the strongest wind recorded was 68 MPH at Sidney at 900 PM April 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: 45-55 mph winds created blizzard conditions across northcentral Nebraska.  Blowing and drifting snow caused icy roads and visibilities near zero. A storm-related fatality occurred when an 18 year-old male was struck and killed by a passing vehicle.

A record breaking late season cold snap generated the latest spring freeze on record over portions of eastern Nebraska as temperatures dipped into the 26-32F degree range.  Some minor crop and fruit tree damage resulted from the cold temperatures.  This was especially true in low lying areas since the cold air tended to drain into those areas.  Damage was generally minor as many of the plants had just begun to emerge and were not particularly succeptible to the cold.

Strong winds combined with snow amounts of generally 8 to 12 inches to create widespread blizzard conditions in the Nebraska panhandle.  Closed and impassable roads became the norm from the evening of April 4 through April 6 due to blowing and drifting snow.  Many automobile accidents occurred as a result of the treacherous conditions, and many power lines were downed due to the weight of snow.  Many livestock were killed by the snow and accompanying cold temperatures because the storm occurred in the calving season.  The highest snow amount was 12 inches in the Wildcat Hills region of Banner County, and the strongest wind recorded was 68 MPH at Sidney at 900 PM April 5.

Surface low pressure developed and strengthened over northern Texas then moved northeast through Missouri and into Illinois.  This combined with a strong upper level low pressure trough which moved out of the Rockies and into the plains generated widespread snow across the region.  Snow fell particularly heavy from mid morning through mid afternoon April 11th over southern Boone...northern Platte... southeast Madison...Stanton...northern Colfax...Cuming...southern Thurston and northern Burt counties.  Snow then decreased during the late afternoon hours, then increased by early evening and continued through much of the night as the main upper level system moved northeast from western Kansas through Nebraska.  By morning April 12th, most of the area had experienced 5 to 9 inches of snow from the system.

An intense low pressure moving slowly from southwest Nebraska toward northeast Nebraska dumped 10 inches of snow in the Nebraska Panhandle. A cold front with very strong northwest winds gusting to around 45 mph produced blizzard conditions across the panhandle. High winds broke down power lines so that some residents were without power for as long as 15 hours. A large 42,000 bushel grain bin was blown down in Hay Springs along with some new construction framing. I-80 was closed to traffic west of Big Springs Saturday afternoon and night.

The hail was propelled by winds around 60 mph.  Crops were severely damaged in the area, especially in western Hall County along with tree damage across the area.

Several irrigation pivots were overturned across this area.  Numerous funnel clouds were reported in northern Fillmore County but none reached the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of western Kimball County received in excess of 5 inches of rain in less than 5 hours, with the remainder of the county and western Cheyenne County averaging more than 2 inches.  The maximum rainfall reported was 7. 5 inches in the southwest corner of the county.  Cropland flooding was widespread throughout both counties, and Lodgepole Creek spilled out of its banks from Bushnell in western Kimball County to Lodgepole in extreme eastern Cheyenne County, affecting several communities.  Two bridges were washed out along Lodgepole Creek--one on a county road north of Dix in eastern Kimball County, and the second on Highway 30 outside of Potter in western Cheyenne County.  While flood waters were reported in Bushnell and Lodgepole, the most severe occurred in Potter, where 4 feet of water inundated numerous homes and businesses, washing away a dozen large propane tanks and washing out 30 feet of Union Pacific Railroad track serving as a dike on Lodgepole Creek.  Numerous county roads in both counties were damaged or washed out, and more than 80 miles of farmland along Lodgepole Creek and Sand Draw were  covered with several feet of flood water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms produced in excess of 7.5 inches of rain over parts of western Kimball County during a 5-hour time frame.  Flooding occurred along Lodgepole Creek from Bushnell east to the county line.

At the height of the storm, near white-out conditions causing poor visibility teamed with treacherous road condtions to close a portion of Interstate 80.  Numerous accidents resulted from the poor driving conditions as 3 to 4 feet drifts were common.  Most schools and several businesses were closed the morning of April 12.Despite the cold and wintry weather, the snow provided much needed and widespread moisture to region.

Flooding on the Missouri River was the result of snowmelt runoff from the James, Vermillion, Big and Little Sioux, and Floyd rivers in South Dakota and northwest Iowa.  In addition, record high releases from Gavins Point Dam added to the flooding.  Lowland flooding of agricultural bottomlands, boat marinas, and some local parks was common along the river east of Blair and points further south.

Thunderstorms along Interstate 80 produced a variety of weather across south-central Nebraska.  Winds from 60 to 75 mph along with hail up to an inch and a half in diameter causes considerable crop and property damage.  Several funnel clouds were spotted in Dawson County.  A well developed wall cloud and a few funnels occurred in Merrick County.

Early afternoon thunderstorms developed between Grand Island and York and moved slowly southeast.  The storms produced very gusty winds and hail up to the size of golf balls.  Several irrigation pivots were overturned across this area.  Numerous funnel clouds were reported in northern Fillmore County but none reached the ground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms with large hail and winds in excess of 60 mph destroyed crops and property in a path across Southwest Nebraska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure developed and strengthened over northern Texas then moved northeast through Missouri and into Illinois.  This combined with a strong upper level low pressure trough which moved out of the Rockies and into the plains generated widespread snow across the region.  Snow fell particularly heavy from mid morning through mid afternoon April 11th over southern Boone...northern Platte... southeast Madison...Stanton...northern Colfax...Cuming...southern Thurston and northern Burt counties.  Snow then decreased during the late afternoon hours, then increased by early evening and continued through much of the night as the main upper level system moved northeast from western Kansas through Nebraska.  By morning April 12th, most of the area had experienced 5 to 9 inches of snow from the system.

A strong low pressure system moved northeast from the central plains through northern Minnesota to eastern Canada.  The tight pressure gradient between the low and a cold Canadian high pressure system which moved south into the plains behind the low generated very strong winds.  Winds gusted over 60 mph over the area.  Some trees were blown down and scattered power outages occurred.

A fast developing thunderstorm brought torrential rain, hail and high winds which damaged several thousand acres of crops.
Lightning strikes nearby knocked out cash register scanners at a supermarket.
Thunderstorm winds caused dozens of trees to be uprooted or damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A record breaking late season cold snap generated the latest spring freeze on record over portions of eastern Nebraska as temperatures dipped into the 26-32F degree range.  Some minor crop and fruit tree damage resulted from the cold temperatures.  This was especially true in low lying areas since the cold air tended to drain into those areas.  Damage was generally minor as many of the plants had just begun to emerge and were not particularly succeptible to the cold.

An intense low pressure moving slowly from southwest Nebraska toward northeast Nebraska dumped 10 inches of snow in the Nebraska Panhandle. A cold front with very strong northwest winds gusting to around 45 mph produced blizzard conditions across the panhandle. High winds broke down power lines so that some residents were without power for as long as 15 hours. A large 42,000 bushel grain bin was blown down in Hay Springs along with some new construction framing. I-80 was closed to traffic west of Big Springs Saturday afternoon and night.

Surface low pressure developed and strengthened over northern Texas then moved northeast through Missouri and into Illinois.  This combined with a strong upper level low pressure trough which moved out of the Rockies and into the plains generated widespread snow across the region.  As warm, moist air was forced to overrun cold arctic air over the area, snow developed during the late evening of April 10th with 4-6 inches of accumulation by 6am CST on April 11th.  Snow was light for much of the morning and early afternoon.  Heavy snow then increased again during the late afternoon and continued through much of the night as the main upper level system moved northeast from western Kansas through Nebraska.  By morning April 12th, most of the area had experienced between 8 and 12 inches of snow from the system.

Aurora took top honors with 14 inches of new snow. A strong low pressure system moved northeast from the central plains through northern Minnesota to eastern Canada.  The tight pressure gradient between the low and a cold Canadian high pressure system which moved south into the plains behind the low generated very strong winds.  Winds gusted over 60 mph over the area.  Some trees were blown down and scattered power outages occurred.

An intense low pressure moving slowly from southwest Nebraska toward northeast Nebraska dumped 10 inches of snow in the Nebraska Panhandle. A cold front with very strong northwest winds gusting to around 45 mph produced blizzard conditions across the panhandle. High winds broke down power lines so that some residents were without power for as long as 15 hours. A large 42,000 bushel grain bin was blown down in Hay Springs along with some new construction framing. I-80 was closed to traffic west of Big Springs Saturday afternoon and night.

Surface low pressure developed and strengthened over northern Texas then moved northeast through Missouri and into Illinois.  This combined with a strong upper level low pressure trough which moved out of the Rockies and into the plains generated widespread snow across the region.  Snow fell particularly heavy from mid morning through mid afternoon April 11th over southern Boone...northern Platte... southeast Madison...Stanton...northern Colfax...Cuming...southern Thurston and northern Burt counties.  Snow then decreased during the late afternoon hours, then increased by early evening and continued through much of the night as the main upper level system moved northeast from western Kansas through Nebraska.  By morning April 12th, most of the area had experienced 5 to 9 inches of snow from the system.

High pressure over the Rockies and low pressure in the Great Lakes Region created a tight pressure gradient which produced northwest winds at 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph.  The winds buffeted the Eastern Nebraska Panhandle as well as Southwest and Northcentral Nebraska.  High velocity winds caused considerable damage in some of the corn fields still unharvested.  Some fields incurred as much as a 15 to 20 bushel per acre decline in yield caused by corn blown off the stalks.

Travel became treacherous across south central Nebraska as freezing drizzle coated area roads with a thin layer of ice.  The freezing drizzle began the afternoon of the 7th, then gave way to light snow by the 9th.  Though amouts were light, the snow and ice likely played a role in several accidents in the region.  Many drivers lost control of their vehicles on the icy roads.

Ground blizzard conditions were created by strong winds blowing over freshly fallen snow.  Visibility of only 8 feet was reported on Nebraska highway 20 from Chadron east to Sheridan County at approximately 2100.  Highway 71 was closed between Kimball and Gering, and Interstate 80 were closed in the vicinity of Kimball due to whiteout conditions with sustained winds of 40 MPH.  Approximately 35 tractor trailers, one bus and 15 cars stayed in the parking lot of a fast food restaurant that remained open to provide safe haven to travellers.  Harrison reported wind gusts of 60 MPH at approximately 1200.  Numerous traffic accidents occurred, including a single car rollover 5 miles west of Bridgeport that injured the driver and caused $3K damage to the car.

Strong wind blew through south central Nebraska the night of October 8.  Sustained wind speeds of 30 to 50 mph were common throughout the night.  Numerous locations reported wind gusts in excess of 60 mph. Sporadic damage was reported in the area due to the prolonged high winds.  A hog confinement facility was destroyed near Loup City and a barn blown over near Alma.  Some power loss was noted, mainly due to damaged tree limbs falling on power lines.

An autumn snowstorm pummeled central and south central Nebraska with record early season snows.  Wind driven snowfall amounts totaled as much as two feet by storms' end.The storms' origin began in the southwest United States with a strong upper level low pressure riding along the southern jet stream.  As the storm pulled northeast  toward Nebraska, colder air slowly filtered into central Nebraska beginning late Friday night.  Snow fell north and west of an Aurora to Franklin line during the day Saturday, while rain continued further southeast.  By evening, all of the area was receiving snow and increasing north winds as the low pressure intensified in northern Oklahoma. Several highways were closed, including Interstate 80, as near blizzard conditiions developed.  Once the snow subsided, the record early season snow totals were tallied.  Guide Rock measured twenty-four inches of snow, Clay Center twenty -three inches and Hastings seventeen inches.  A fifty-mile wide swath of snow in excess of fifteen inches fell from near Alma to York.  Amounts further north averaged from four to eight inches. The heary, wet snow was responsible for many power outages in the area as tree limbs broke and fell on power lines.  At one point, the town of Hardy had no power, and could not be accessed by vehicle due to the snow. Numerous schools and businesses remained closed several days following the storm.  Many highways, including Interstate 80, were closed at the height  of the storm.  On highway 136 east of Alma, road crews worked for ten hours carvning through a ten foot drift which covered the road.  Record cold accompanied the snow as temperatures dropped to the single digits the morning of the 26th.

Many drivers lost control of their vehicles on the icy roads.

A major winter storm moved across southwest and northcentral Nebraska dumping from 4 to 12 inches of snow.  Sustained winds at 40 mph with gusts to near 60 mph created blowing and drifting snow.  The storm began as rain and then changed over to snow as temperatures fell.  Ice and snow collected on power lines and trees causing considerable damage to tree limbs and scattered power outages.  Interstate 80 was closed to traffic along with other roads in the area due to blowing and drifting snow and icy conditions.  However, there were still numerous traffic accidents due to slick roads and poor visibilities.  Many school and community activities were cancelled or postponed.

A major early season snowstorm struck the area.  A heavy wet snowfall of 6 to 14 inches fell on trees, many of which were still fully or partially leafed, and caused extensive damage and/or total destruction.  At least 205,000 residents in the affected area were without power just after  the storm, many of the outages lasted for several days.  Omaha Public Power District estimated that it was the worst outage in 50 years.  Nearly 85% of the trees in the Omaha area and 25% of the trees in the Lincoln area sustained damage or were totally destroyed.  Many emergency shelters in and around the Omaha and Lincoln areas were opened for use by those who suffered a hardship from the storm.

An autumn snowstorm pummeled central and south central Nebraska with record early season snows.  Wind driven snowfall amounts totaled as much as two feet by storms' end.The storms' origin began in the southwest United States with a strong upper level low pressure riding along the southern jet stream.  As the storm pulled northeast  toward Nebraska, colder air slowly filtered into central Nebraska beginning late Friday night.  Snow fell north and west of an Aurora to Franklin line during the day Saturday, while rain continued further southeast.  By evening, all of the area was receiving snow and increasing north winds as the low pressure intensified in northern Oklahoma. Several highways were closed, including Interstate 80, as near blizzard conditiions developed.  Once the snow subsided, the record early season snow totals were tallied.  Guide Rock measured twenty-four inches of snow, Clay Center twenty -three inches and Hastings seventeen inches.  A fifty-mile wide swath of snow in excess of fifteen inches fell from near Alma to York.  Amounts further north averaged from four to eight inches. The heary, wet snow was responsible for many power outages in the area as tree limbs broke and fell on power lines.  At one point, the town of Hardy had no power, and could not be accessed by vehicle due to the snow. Numerous schools and businesses remained closed several days following the storm.  Many highways, including Interstate 80, were closed at the height  of the storm.  On highway 136 east of Alma, road crews worked for ten hours carvning through a ten foot drift which covered the road.  Record cold accompanied the snow as temperatures dropped to the single digits the morning of the 26th.

High pressure over the Rockies and low pressure in the Great Lakes Region created a tight pressure gradient which produced northwest winds at 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph.  The winds buffeted the Eastern Nebraska Panhandle as well as Southwest and Northcentral Nebraska.  High velocity winds caused considerable damage in some of the corn fields still unharvested.  Some fields incurred as much as a 15 to 20 bushel per acre decline in yield caused by corn blown off the stalks.

Travel became treacherous across south central Nebraska as freezing drizzle coated area roads with a thin layer of ice.  The freezing drizzle began the afternoon of the 7th, then gave way to light snow by the 9th.  Though amouts were light, the snow and ice likely played a role in several accidents in the region.  Many drivers lost control of their vehicles on the icy roads.

A steady light freezing rain covered area roads with a layer of ice, resulting in several accidents into early Monday morning.  The slick roads may have played a role in a two-vehicle crash near Bruning the morning of the 21st.  One person died in the accident.

Strong wind blew through south central Nebraska the night of October 8.  Sustained wind speeds of 30 to 50 mph were common throughout the night.  Numerous locations reported wind gusts in excess of 60 mph. Sporadic damage was reported in the area due to the prolonged high winds.  A hog confinement facility was destroyed near Loup City and a barn blown over near Alma.  Some power loss was noted, mainly due to damaged tree limbs falling on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved across southwest and northcentral Nebraska dumping from 4 to 12 inches of snow.  Sustained winds at 40 mph with gusts to near 60 mph created blowing and drifting snow.  The storm began as rain and then changed over to snow as temperatures fell.  Ice and snow collected on power lines and trees causing considerable damage to tree limbs and scattered power outages.  Interstate 80 was closed to traffic along with other roads in the area due to blowing and drifting snow and icy conditions.  However, there were still numerous traffic accidents due to slick roads and poor visibilities.  Many school and community activities were cancelled or postponed.

Ground blizzard conditions were created by strong winds blowing over freshly fallen snow.  Visibility of only 8 feet was reported on Nebraska highway 20 from Chadron east to Sheridan County at approximately 2100.  Highway 71 was closed between Kimball and Gering, and Interstate 80 were closed in the vicinity of Kimball due to whiteout conditions with sustained winds of 40 MPH.  Approximately 35 tractor trailers, one bus and 15 cars stayed in the parking lot of a fast food restaurant that remained open to provide safe haven to travellers.  Harrison reported wind gusts of 60 MPH at approximately 1200.  Numerous traffic accidents occurred, including a single car rollover 5 miles west of Bridgeport that injured the driver and caused $3K damage to the car.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds of 62 MPH blew the roof off a construction company building at 1746 and deposited the roof 100 yards away.  Winds also damaged several business signs, and split a tree truck which then partially blocked a residential street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds persisted across the area for several hours causing minor structural and tree damage.  A few power outages also resulted.

Strong south winds gusting to 60 mph at times ahead of an approaching cold front caused considerable damage to corn fields and property in southwest Nebraska.  The destructive winds caused corn ears to become dislodged and fall to the ground so that harvesting the downed ears will be impossible.  Estimates indicate losses to farmers ranging from 5 to 20 percent.  A  tree was blown over causing a power outage in the south part of North Platte.  There were also scattered reports of wind damage to siding, shingles and other residential property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowpacked and icy roads contributed to many vehicular accidents, one of which killed a 73-year-old man and his 59-year-old wife on Highway 71 when a semitrailer truck lost control, crossed the center line and struck their car head-on.  Both vehicles caught fire and were totaled.  The numerous other accidents involved property damage and some minor injuries but no additional fatalities.

An early season blizzard dumped up to 12 inches of snow in areas of the Nebraska Panhandle, downing power poles and power lines as well as making many roads impassable.  Wet, wind-driven snow damaged unharvested milo, corn and sunflower fields, and many motorists were stranded on impassable roads or when vehicles slid off roads.  Scotts Bluff county spent an estimated $5K in overtime trying to keep roads passable.  High school athletic events were postponed, as were many other events.  By the evening  of the 24th, approximately half of the homes in Kimball were without power due to fallen power poles and lines.     The following occurrences were documented as occurring on the 24th.  In addition to numerous minor traffic accidents, a Utah man suffered head injuries when a semi-tractor with two trailers jackknifed on ice and slid off a bridge over the Potter interchange of Interstate 80 at 1231, totaling the tractor and first trailer in addition to closing the interstate for five hours.  A Kansas man suffered minor facial injuries when he lost control of his car on ice and slid into a bridge at the east Sidney interchange of Interstate 80 at 1430.  A cattle truck overturned two miles south of Sidney on Highway 19 at 1750, causing $50K damage to the trailer and killing 17 cattle.  A semi-tractor on Highway 2 tipped over on its side in Alliance.     The following occurrences were documented as occurring on the 25th.  Interstate 80 was closed from east of the Panhandle to the Wyoming border.  All county roads in Banner County were closed.  Highway 71 was closed from Crawford to the Colorado border.  55 cattle were killed at approximately 0300 north of Alliance when they wandered onto railroad tracks and were struck by a westbound Burlington-Northern Santa Fe train travelling at 50 MPH in conditions of near-zero visibility; the value of the cattle was estimated at $55K and no damage occurred to the train or tracks.  A pileup involving five vehicles occurred 17 miles south of Chadron on Highway 385 before the highway was closed north and south of Chadron.  Kimball received an estimated $20K damage from fallen power lines and poles as well as fallen trees.  100 to 150 power poles were downed in the vicinity of Kimball, and Kimball County was declared a disaster area.     The following occurrence was documented as occurring on the 26th.  A Georgia man suffered head and hand injuries when the semi-tractor and trailer he was driving jackknifed and was struck by another semi-tractor and trailer at 0837; occurred on westbound Interstate 80 at the Brownson interchange.

A strong low pressure produced 3 to 5 inches of snow across the Northeast Nebraska Panhandle and Northcentral Nebraska.  Northwest winds at 25 to 40 mph with some stronger gusts produced blowing and drifing of snow and visibilities down to zero at times.  The strong winds and temperatures around 20 degrees produced wind chill values ranging from 15 to 25 below zero.  The winter weather caused some school closings and cancellation of activities.

High pressure over the Rockies and low pressure in the Great Lakes Region created a tight pressure gradient which produced northwest winds at 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph.  The winds buffeted the Eastern Nebraska Panhandle as well as Southwest and Northcentral Nebraska.  High velocity winds caused considerable damage in some of the corn fields still unharvested.  Some fields incurred as much as a 15 to 20 bushel per acre decline in yield caused by corn blown off the stalks.

An autumn snowstorm pummeled central and south central Nebraska with record early season snows.  Wind driven snowfall amounts totaled as much as two feet by storms' end.The storms' origin began in the southwest United States with a strong upper level low pressure riding along the southern jet stream.  As the storm pulled northeast  toward Nebraska, colder air slowly filtered into central Nebraska beginning late Friday night.  Snow fell north and west of an Aurora to Franklin line during the day Saturday, while rain continued further southeast.  By evening, all of the area was receiving snow and increasing north winds as the low pressure intensified in northern Oklahoma. Several highways were closed, including Interstate 80, as near blizzard conditiions developed.  Once the snow subsided, the record early season snow totals were tallied.  Guide Rock measured twenty-four inches of snow, Clay Center twenty -three inches and Hastings seventeen inches.  A fifty-mile wide swath of snow in excess of fifteen inches fell from near Alma to York.  Amounts further north averaged from four to eight inches. The heary, wet snow was responsible for many power outages in the area as tree limbs broke and fell on power lines.  At one point, the town of Hardy had no power, and could not be accessed by vehicle due to the snow. Numerous schools and businesses remained closed several days following the storm.  Many highways, including Interstate 80, were closed at the height  of the storm.  On highway 136 east of Alma, road crews worked for ten hours carvning through a ten foot drift which covered the road.  Record cold accompanied the snow as temperatures dropped to the single digits the morning of the 26th.

A large severe thunderstorm pummelled late season crops across a portion of south central Nebraska.  The storm intensified as it moved southeast from north of Palmer dropping large hail along its path.  The area from western Nance and Merrick counties south to western Clay county sustained the most damage.  The storm then blew through extreme southern Nebraska with winds in excess of 60 mph dropping limbs and power poles in Thayer county.

A steady light freezing rain covered area roads with a layer of ice, resulting in several accidents into early Monday morning.  The slick roads may have played a role in a two-vehicle crash near Bruning the morning of the 21st.  One person died in the accident.

Travel became treacherous across south central Nebraska as freezing drizzle coated area roads with a thin layer of ice.  The freezing drizzle began the afternoon of the 7th, then gave way to light snow by the 9th.  Though amouts were light, the snow and ice likely played a role in several accidents in the region.  Many drivers lost control of their vehicles on the icy roads.

A major winter storm moved across southwest and northcentral Nebraska dumping from 4 to 12 inches of snow.  Sustained winds at 40 mph with gusts to near 60 mph created blowing and drifting snow.  The storm began as rain and then changed over to snow as temperatures fell.  Ice and snow collected on power lines and trees causing considerable damage to tree limbs and scattered power outages.  Interstate 80 was closed to traffic along with other roads in the area due to blowing and drifting snow and icy conditions.  However, there were still numerous traffic accidents due to slick roads and poor visibilities.  Many school and community activities were cancelled or postponed.

At one point, the town of Hardy had no power, and could not be accessed by vehicle due to the snow. Numerous schools and businesses remained closed several days following the storm.  Many highways, including Interstate 80, were closed at the height  of the storm.  On highway 136 east of Alma, road crews worked for ten hours carvning through a ten foot drift which covered the road.  Record cold accompanied the snow as temperatures dropped to the single digits the morning of the 26th.

Travel became treacherous across south central Nebraska as freezing drizzle coated area roads with a thin layer of ice.  The freezing drizzle began the afternoon of the 7th, then gave way to light snow by the 9th.  Though amouts were light, the snow and ice likely played a role in several accidents in the region.  Many drivers lost control of their vehicles on the icy roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated gust to 60 mph. Trees and branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Rockies and low pressure in the Great Lakes Region created a tight pressure gradient which produced northwest winds at 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph.  The winds buffeted the Eastern Nebraska Panhandle as well as Southwest and Northcentral Nebraska.  High velocity winds caused considerable damage in some of the corn fields still unharvested.  Some fields incurred as much as a 15 to 20 bushel per acre decline in yield caused by corn blown off the stalks.

Some fields incurred as much as a 15 to 20 bushel per acre decline in yield caused by corn blown off the stalks.

A major early season snowstorm struck the area.  A heavy wet snowfall of 6 to 14 inches fell on trees, many of which were still fully or partially leafed, and caused extensive damage and/or total destruction.  At least 205,000 residents in the affected area were without power just after  the storm, many of the outages lasted for several days.  Omaha Public Power District estimated that it was the worst outage in 50 years.  Nearly 85% of the trees in the Omaha area and 25% of the trees in the Lincoln area sustained damage or were totally destroyed.  Many emergency shelters in and around the Omaha and Lincoln areas were opened for use by those who suffered a hardship from the storm.

Snowfall up to 1 foot  combined with north winds 50 to 60 mph created widespread blizzard conditions with near zero visibility and wind chills to 20 degrees below zero.  Specific snowfall amounts were Haigler 12 inches, Benkelman 12, Stratton 7, Trenton 6, Palisade 6, Culbertson 8, McCook 10, Red Willow Dam 4.  All activities were postponed or cancelled.  Roads became impassable and were closed.  Many schools were closed for 1 to 2 days afterwards.

Strong winds were widespread across the area causing minor tree and structural damage and scattered power outages.  In Sarpy county, 4 miles east of Gretna, a garage door buckled and siding was stripped from a house.  In Cedar county near Fordyce, a steel pig barn was sent airborne by the strong winds.

A major winter storm moved across southwest and northcentral Nebraska dumping from 4 to 12 inches of snow.  Sustained winds at 40 mph with gusts to near 60 mph created blowing and drifting snow.  The storm began as rain and then changed over to snow as temperatures fell.  Ice and snow collected on power lines and trees causing considerable damage to tree limbs and scattered power outages.  Interstate 80 was closed to traffic along with other roads in the area due to blowing and drifting snow and icy conditions.  However, there were still numerous traffic accidents due to slick roads and poor visibilities.  Many school and community activities were cancelled or postponed.

An autumn snowstorm pummeled central and south central Nebraska with record early season snows.  Wind driven snowfall amounts totaled as much as two feet by storms' end.The storms' origin began in the southwest United States with a strong upper level low pressure riding along the southern jet stream.  As the storm pulled northeast  toward Nebraska, colder air slowly filtered into central Nebraska beginning late Friday night.  Snow fell north and west of an Aurora to Franklin line during the day Saturday, while rain continued further southeast.  By evening, all of the area was receiving snow and increasing north winds as the low pressure intensified in northern Oklahoma. Several highways were closed, including Interstate 80, as near blizzard conditiions developed.  Once the snow subsided, the record early season snow totals were tallied.  Guide Rock measured twenty-four inches of snow, Clay Center twenty -three inches and Hastings seventeen inches.  A fifty-mile wide swath of snow in excess of fifteen inches fell from near Alma to York.  Amounts further north averaged from four to eight inches. The heary, wet snow was responsible for many power outages in the area as tree limbs broke and fell on power lines.  At one point, the town of Hardy had no power, and could not be accessed by vehicle due to the snow. Numerous schools and businesses remained closed several days following the storm.  Many highways, including Interstate 80, were closed at the height  of the storm.  On highway 136 east of Alma, road crews worked for ten hours carvning through a ten foot drift which covered the road.  Record cold accompanied the snow as temperatures dropped to the single digits the morning of the 26th.

Ground blizzard conditions were created by strong winds blowing over freshly fallen snow.  Visibility of only 8 feet was reported on Nebraska highway 20 from Chadron east to Sheridan County at approximately 2100.  Highway 71 was closed between Kimball and Gering, and Interstate 80 were closed in the vicinity of Kimball due to whiteout conditions with sustained winds of 40 MPH.  Approximately 35 tractor trailers, one bus and 15 cars stayed in the parking lot of a fast food restaurant that remained open to provide safe haven to travellers.  Harrison reported wind gusts of 60 MPH at approximately 1200.  Numerous traffic accidents occurred, including a single car rollover 5 miles west of Bridgeport that injured the driver and caused $3K damage to the car.

Strong south winds gusting to 60 mph at times ahead of an approaching cold front caused considerable damage to corn fields and property in southwest Nebraska.  The destructive winds caused corn ears to become dislodged and fall to the ground so that harvesting the downed ears will be impossible.  Estimates indicate losses to farmers ranging from 5 to 20 percent.  A  tree was blown over causing a power outage in the south part of North Platte.  There were also scattered reports of wind damage to siding, shingles and other residential property.

This caused lowland rural flooding along the Maple Creek and county road damages from the smaller feeder streams in the same basin.  This same storm dumped 3 to 5 inches of rain in the Papillion Creek Basin in and near Omaha causing numerous pockets of urban flooding and closing streets in several locations in Douglas county and within the Omaha city limits.

Wind driven snowfall amounts totaled as much as two feet by storms' end.The storms' origin began in the southwest United States with a strong upper level low pressure riding along the southern jet stream.  As the storm pulled northeast  toward Nebraska, colder air slowly filtered into central Nebraska beginning late Friday night.  Snow fell north and west of an Aurora to Franklin line during the day Saturday, while rain continued further southeast.  By evening, all of the area was receiving snow and increasing north winds as the low pressure intensified in northern Oklahoma. Several highways were closed, including Interstate 80, as near blizzard conditiions developed.  Once the snow subsided, the record early season snow totals were tallied.  Guide Rock measured twenty-four inches of snow, Clay Center twenty -three inches and Hastings seventeen inches.  A fifty-mile wide swath of snow in excess of fifteen inches fell from near Alma to York.  Amounts further north averaged from four to eight inches. The heary, wet snow was responsible for many power outages in the area as tree limbs broke and fell on power lines.  At one point, the town of Hardy had no power, and could not be accessed by vehicle due to the snow. Numerous schools and businesses remained closed several days following the storm.  Many highways, including Interstate 80, were closed at the height  of the storm.  On highway 136 east of Alma, road crews worked for ten hours carvning through a ten foot drift which covered the road.  Record cold accompanied the snow as temperatures dropped to the single digits the morning of the 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure produced 3 to 5 inches of snow across the Northeast Nebraska Panhandle and Northcentral Nebraska.  Northwest winds at 25 to 40 mph with some stronger gusts produced blowing and drifing of snow and visibilities down to zero at times.  The strong winds and temperatures around 20 degrees produced wind chill values ranging from 15 to 25 below zero.  The winter weather caused some school closings and cancellation of activities.

Strong wind blew through south central Nebraska the night of October 8.  Sustained wind speeds of 30 to 50 mph were common throughout the night.  Numerous locations reported wind gusts in excess of 60 mph. Sporadic damage was reported in the area due to the prolonged high winds.  A hog confinement facility was destroyed near Loup City and a barn blown over near Alma.  Some power loss was noted, mainly due to damaged tree limbs falling on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind blew through south central Nebraska the night of October 8.  Sustained wind speeds of 30 to 50 mph were common throughout the night.  Numerous locations reported wind gusts in excess of 60 mph. Sporadic damage was reported in the area due to the prolonged high winds. High pressure over the Rockies and low pressure in the Great Lakes Region created a tight pressure gradient which produced northwest winds at 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph.  The winds buffeted the Eastern Nebraska Panhandle as well as Southwest and Northcentral Nebraska.  High velocity winds caused considerable damage in some of the corn fields still unharvested.  Some fields incurred as much as a 15 to 20 bushel per acre decline in yield caused by corn blown off the stalks.

A major early season snowstorm struck the area.  A heavy wet snowfall of 6 to 14 inches fell on trees, many of which were still fully or partially leafed, and caused extensive damage and/or total destruction.  At least 205,000 residents in the affected area were without power just after  the storm, many of the outages lasted for several days.  Omaha Public Power District estimated that it was the worst outage in 50 years.  Nearly 85% of the trees in the Omaha area and 25% of the trees in the Lincoln area sustained damage or were totally destroyed.  Many emergency shelters in and around the Omaha and Lincoln areas were opened for use by those who suffered a hardship from the storm.

Travel became treacherous across south central Nebraska as freezing drizzle coated area roads with a thin layer of ice.  The freezing drizzle began the afternoon of the 7th, then gave way to light snow by the 9th.  Though amouts were light, the snow and ice likely played a role in several accidents in the region.  Many drivers lost control of their vehicles on the icy roads.

A steady light freezing rain covered area roads with a layer of ice, resulting in several accidents into early Monday morning.  The slick roads may have played a role in a two-vehicle crash near Bruning the morning of the 21st.  One person died in the accident.

Snowpacked and icy roads contributed to many vehicular accidents, one of which killed a 73-year-old man and his 59-year-old wife on Highway 71 when a semitrailer truck lost control, crossed the center line and struck their car head-on.  Both vehicles caught fire and were totaled.  The numerous other accidents involved property damage and some minor injuries but no additional fatalities.
